Patta Barawari
Family Home

Architect Brief & Design Direction

A timeless Himachali family home inspired by Portuguese country estates and English countryside interiors, adapted for Solan climate, family living and low-maintenance ownership.

LocationPatta Barawari, Solan District
Built-up area3,500–4,000 sq ft
Outdoor living800–1,000 sq ft
Budget target₹75 lakh–₹1 Cr incl. interiors
Design mandate: Build a warm, practical family estate — not a resort, not a glass villa. The traditional Himachali kitchen, modern support kitchen and ground floor family space should be the heart of the home.

1. How The House Will Actually Be Used

Daily / regular use

  • Parents will primarily use the ground floor throughout the year.
  • Traditional Himachali kitchen will be the main daily-use space.
  • Modern support kitchen will sit adjacent to the traditional kitchen.
  • Family dining and baithak area will be used daily.
  • Existing family house can continue serving some functions including pooja room and additional accommodation.

Occasional use

  • Master bedroom and Zain's bedroom will be on the first floor.
  • Guest rooms on the third floor will be used occasionally.
  • The full family is expected to use the house for approximately 60 days per year.
  • Priority: practicality, durability and ease of maintenance.

2. Project Vision

Core idea

Create a family home that feels warm, elegant and rooted in Himachal, while borrowing the best proportions and softness of Portuguese / European country homes.

  • Multi-generational family use
  • Comfortable for parents
  • Beautiful but not flashy
  • Easy to maintain when not fully occupied
  • Designed to age well over 20–30 years

Design keywords

  • Warm
  • Timeless
  • Natural light
  • Kitchen-centred
  • Covered verandahs
  • Stone + wood + plaster
  • Low maintenance
One-line brief: A timeless Himachali family home inspired by Portuguese country estates and English countryside interiors, centred around a large traditional Himachali kitchen with chulha, adjacent modern support kitchen, family baithak/dining space, abundant natural light, covered verandahs and long-term practicality.

3. Site Context & Planning

Site: Family-owned land in Patta Barawari, Solan District, near Tapalori Durga Temple area.

Existing house: The old family house remains on the property and does not need to be demolished. The new house should complement it rather than duplicate every function.

Site priorities

  • Orient main family spaces toward best light and views
  • Create easy access between house, verandah and garden
  • Keep landscaping natural and low-maintenance
  • Allow parking and simple circulation
  • Preserve scope for fruit trees / kitchen garden

Area direction

ComponentTarget
Built-up area3,500–4,000 sq ft
Garden / outdoor living800–1,000 sq ft
Overall developed experienceApprox. 4,500–5,000 sq ft
Budget target₹75 lakh–₹1 Cr incl. interiors

4. Exterior Reference Board

Use

  • Warm plaster walls: cream / off-white / soft beige
  • Local stone detailing at plinth and landscape walls
  • Sloping roof with deep overhangs
  • Covered verandahs and shaded outdoor sitting
  • Arches at selected feature points

Avoid

  • Full glass façades
  • Too many roof junctions
  • Overly formal mansion look
  • High-maintenance exterior timber everywhere

5. Windows & Doors

Principle: Lots of light, not lots of glass.

Recommended

  • Large framed windows
  • French doors opening to verandah/garden
  • Deep window reveals
  • Dark bronze, walnut or sage green frames
  • Operable windows for cross-ventilation

Limit / avoid

  • Continuous glass walls
  • Large glass roofs
  • Custom panels that are hard to replace locally
  • Overly industrial black-frame look

6. Interior Mood Board

Interior direction

English countryside interiors with Himachali warmth: cream walls, warm timber, linen curtains, traditional lighting, comfortable furniture and family spaces that feel lived-in.

7. Traditional Himachali Kitchen + Modern Support Kitchen

Most important area

The ground floor kitchen complex should be the emotional centre of the house and the most-used space through the year.

  • Traditional Himachali kitchen / chulha zone: primary family cooking area with proper chulha provision, traditional cooking platform and ventilation/chimney planning.
  • Floor-level dish washing area: separate low-height / sitting-on-floor washing zone for utensils, designed the way it is actually used in village homes.
  • Modern support kitchen: placed immediately on the other side / adjacent to the traditional kitchen, not far away.
  • Modern standing washing sink: the modern kitchen should include a normal counter-height sink for standing dish washing and daily convenience.
  • No large modern island/table: avoid a big central standing island in the modern kitchen; keep it as a clean support kitchen with counters on the side.
  • Kitchen storage / pantry: large storage for grains, utensils, pickles, bulk supplies and seasonal items.
  • Dining + sitting area: large family baithak-style dining/sitting space connected to the kitchen; not just a formal dining table.
  • Outdoor connection: direct link to verandah, garden and utility/washing area.
Budget rule: If trade-offs are required, prioritize traditional kitchen, chulha zone, modern support kitchen, pantry, floor-level washing zone and family baithak/dining space before formal living areas or decorative features.

8. Flooring & Material Palette

Material palette

ElementDirection
FloorsPremium wood-look porcelain / vitrified tiles in warm oak or walnut tones. Herringbone pattern in key family areas and wide-plank format in bedrooms. Visual appearance should be similar to Dubai house flooring, but with the durability and moisture resistance of tile. Avoid natural timber flooring.
WallsWarm ivory, cream, soft beige
WindowsDark bronze, walnut or sage green frames
StoneLocal Himachal stone accents; avoid polished stone everywhere
DoorsTraditional panelled wood appearance
Flooring principle: The flooring should look like warm timber, not like standard glossy tile. Use high-end matte wood-look porcelain/vitrified tiles with realistic grain and texture. This gives the visual warmth of wood while avoiding swelling, warping, termites and moisture damage in Himachal conditions.

9. Floor-by-Floor Space Program

FloorSpaces RequiredDesign Priority
Ground Floor Traditional Himachali kitchen with chulha, modern support kitchen on the other side, modern standing washing sink, no large central island/table in modern kitchen, floor-level sitting dish-washing area, kitchen storage/pantry, dining + baithak-style sitting area, separate washroom and bath area, washing/utility area, verandah and garden connection. Kitchen-centric family floor; most important everyday-use zone for parents and family gatherings.
First Floor Two bedrooms with attached bathrooms: master bedroom and Zain bedroom. Master suite should include dressing and bathroom; Zain bedroom can have a smaller attached bathroom. Family seating/living area, balcony and washing/laundry area. Comfort, privacy, views and family sitting.
Third Floor / Upper Floor Two guest bedrooms with attached bathrooms including bath areas. Large terrace instead of a living room. Guest accommodation and terrace experience. No separate living room required here.
Outdoor Garden, verandah seating, outdoor dining, fruit trees and kitchen garden. Usable, low-maintenance outdoor living.

10. Garden & Outdoor Living

Target

800–1,000 sq ft usable outdoor living area connected to the kitchen, dining and verandah.

  • Small lawn
  • Outdoor dining
  • Covered verandah
  • Fruit trees
  • Kitchen garden
  • Low-maintenance native planting

Avoid

  • Large formal lawns needing constant care
  • Water features
  • Plants needing daily maintenance
  • Over-designed resort landscaping

11. What To Avoid

Avoid designing

  • Excessive glazing
  • Double-height glass walls
  • Large glass roofs and skylights
  • Resort-style architecture
  • Complex roof intersections
  • High-maintenance exterior detailing
  • Imported custom façade systems
  • Overly modern designs disconnected from Himachal climate
  • Spaces that look impressive but remain unused most of the year

Architect note

The goal is a timeless family home that will be comfortable and practical for the next 30–40 years. Design decisions should prioritize usability, durability and ease of maintenance over visual drama.

Guiding principle: Lots of light, not lots of glass.

12. Maintenance Philosophy

Design for long-term ownership

  • Durable finishes
  • Easy cleaning
  • Weather-resistant construction
  • Simple roof drainage
  • Materials available locally for repairs
  • Low-maintenance landscaping

Special attention

  • Waterproofing of roof and balconies
  • Gutter access for cleaning
  • Window seal quality
  • Ventilation when house is closed
  • Water storage and pressure pump planning

13. Budget Guidance

Target project budget: ₹75 lakh – ₹1.0 crore including interiors.

Priority SpendReason
Traditional + modern kitchen and pantryHeart of the home and most used family area
Windows and doorsDefines light, views and character
Flooring and materialsCreates timeless interior feel
BathroomsLong-term comfort and durability
Verandahs and gardenHigh enjoyment value for family gatherings
Lower priority: decorative ceilings throughout, excessive glazing, complex roofs, oversized rooms and luxury features with high maintenance requirements.

14. Architect Deliverables Required

Concept stage

  • Site plan
  • Floor plans
  • 2–3 elevation options
  • Basic 3D exterior views
  • Material palette
  • Initial cost direction

Detailed stage

  • Structural coordination
  • Window and door schedule
  • Electrical and plumbing layout
  • Kitchen and bathroom layout
  • BOQ estimate
  • Landscape concept
Prepared for architect discussion – Patta Barawari Family Home
